6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

In “Interviewing, Part 3,” the focus shifts from the structural flaws of interviews to practical signal detection for both sides. For engineers, the best indicator of talent is not reciting memorized “correct” answers but reasoning effectively under unfamiliar constraints — pushing into variants like clock drift or aggressive retries reveals first-principles thinking over pattern-matching. Specificity serves as truth serum: genuine experience produces concrete, textured details (e.g., exact deployment metrics and fixes), while inflated claims yield smooth generalities. Positive signals include comfort admitting errors and updating approaches on the spot, plus clear calibration of known vs. unknown knowledge; red flags are defensiveness, vague responses that stay vague under probing, and inability to discuss trade-offs. For engineering managers, signal emerges in how they narrate people — especially failures and disagreements — revealing whether they take ownership or self-serve. Candidates, in turn, should read interviewers’ questions and inconsistencies for hidden team issues, unclear authority, or incoherent company narratives. The piece emphasizes that real signal is behavioral and narrative, not performative.
Parts 1 and 2 explained why interviews are proxy-driven and risk-averse by construction. That leaves the practical question both sides actually care about: once you’re in the room, what should you be looking for — and what should you be showing?
Reasoning under unfamiliarity beats the “correct” answer
For engineers, the single most overrated thing to evaluate is whether someone arrives at the right final answer. The much more useful thing to evaluate is what happens when they don’t already know it.
Say you ask a candidate to design a rate limiter for an API. If they’ve built one before, they’ll recite it, and you’ll learn almost nothing except that they’ve built one before. The interview gets interesting the moment you push into a variant they haven’t seen — multiple regions, clock drift between nodes, a client that can retry aggressively enough to defeat whatever you just designed. Watch what happens next. Do they reason from first principles about what the constraint actually is, or do they reach for pattern-matched vocabulary (“we’d just use a distributed cache”) without being able to say what problem the cache solves? The candidate who says “I think token buckets handle bursts better than fixed windows, but I’m not sure how that interacts with clock drift across regions — let me think through it” is giving you a live look at their reasoning process. The candidate who has memorized the token-bucket algorithm but can’t explain why it exists is giving you a look at their memory, which is a much less useful thing to be renting.
Specificity is a marker of truth, in both directions
Real experience tends to produce concrete, oddly specific detail. Fabricated or inflated experience tends to produce smooth generalities, because generalities are what’s left when the specific texture of actually having done the thing is missing.
If someone says “I improved deployment reliability by implementing better CI/CD practices,” that’s a sentence that could describe almost anything, including nothing. If someone says “we were doing four manual deploys a week, each one needing someone to babysit a Jenkins job at 6pm, and I moved us to a pipeline where a failing integration test blocked the merge instead of blocking the deploy — cut our rollback rate from something like one in five to close to zero,” that’s a sentence that could only come from having actually lived through the specific shape of that problem. The details aren’t decoration. They’re evidence.
This cuts both ways, and it’s worth candidates internalizing as much as interviewers. If you’re preparing answers and they come out smooth and general, that’s not a sign your answer is polished — it’s a sign it doesn’t have enough real material in it yet, and a good interviewer will notice the smoothness and push, not be reassured by it.
What good engineering signal looks like, positive and negative
On the positive side: comfort being wrong in the room. A candidate who tries an approach, gets challenged on it, and says “actually, you’re right, that breaks under load — let me redo this” is showing you something better than correctness: they’re showing you they update. Also positive: a clean separation between “I know this” and “I’d need to check this,” stated without embarrassment. That separation is what calibration looks like in real time, and it’s the single best predictor of whether someone will make good calls later when nobody’s watching them get evaluated.
On the negative side: defensiveness under challenge is the clearest red flag there is, more informative than almost any technical gap. If a candidate can’t explain the trade-offs in a decision from their own past work — not just what they did, but why they didn’t do the alternative — that’s often a sign the decision wasn’t really theirs, or wasn’t examined even when it was. Watch also for answers that get vaguer, not more specific, the deeper you probe. Confident vagueness that improves under a first follow-up and collapses under a second is a distinct pattern worth learning to recognize.
EM signal lives almost entirely in how people are narrated
Engineering manager interviews are mostly conversations about other people, which makes them harder to fake well and harder to evaluate well.
Ask a candidate to describe someone who failed on their team, and listen to where the story puts the responsibility. A manager who says “they just weren’t a strong performer” and stops there is giving you a thin story. A manager who says “I put them on a project that needed someone comfortable with ambiguity, and they needed structure — that was my read error, and by the time I noticed, I’d already put them a quarter behind, so the PIP that followed was partly cleanup for my own mis-assignment” is showing you something rarer: a credit-and-blame story that isn’t self-serving. That second story is also more useful to you as an interviewer, because it tells you how this person will narrate the *next* failure on your team, including the ones that are partly their fault.
The same instinct applies to how a candidate describes disagreeing with their own manager. Someone who has never disagreed with a manager, or whose every disagreement story ends with them being obviously right, is probably editing. Someone who can describe a time they pushed back and lost, and what they did afterward — quietly complied while flagging the risk, escalated once and then dropped it, or changed their own mind on reflection — is showing you how they’ll actually behave inside your own reporting chain, not just how they’d like to be seen.
What candidates should be reading off the interviewers
Signal runs the other direction too, and candidates often under-read it.
The questions an interviewer asks reveal what’s actually keeping them up at night. If three separate interviewers, independently, ask some version of “how do you handle a teammate who’s checked out,” that’s not a coincidence — that’s the team telling you, without meaning to, that they currently have someone checked out and don’t know what to do about it. If a hiring manager is vague about who they actually report to, or answers “how much authority do you have over headcount” with something evasive, that vagueness is itself the answer, and it’s usually more reliable than whatever they say next.
Inconsistency across interviewers is its own signal, and an underrated one. If one interviewer describes the team’s velocity as “we ship fast and figure it out,” and another describes the same team as “we’re pretty deliberate, lots of review,” that’s not two people with different vocabulary — it’s a company that doesn’t have a coherent internal story about itself, and organizations without a coherent internal story are usually not far from having incoherent priorities day to day. A candidate who’s only listening for whether they’re being offered the job is missing the fact that the room is also describing, unintentionally, what it’s actually like to work there.
Part 4 turns to the ethics underneath all of this signal-reading: where honest framing ends and misrepresentation begins, on both sides of the table, and why overclaiming is self-defeating even when it appears to work.
